title: Flexible Working Regulations 2014
chapters: SI 2014/1398 · In Force 30 June 2014 · Made under ERA 1996 s.80F–80I · Amended by Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 (from 6 April 2024) · Supplemented by ACAS Code of Practice 5 (from 6 April 2024)
desc: The Flexible Working Regulations 2014 replaced the statutory procedure for flexible working requests with a duty on the employer to deal with requests in a reasonable manner. The Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 further reformed the framework from 6 April 2024: removing the 26-week qualifying period (day-one right), allowing two requests per 12-month period (previously one), reducing the decision period from 3 months to 2 months, removing the requirement for the employee to explain the effect of the change on the employer, and requiring the employer to consult with the employee before refusing. The ACAS Code of Practice 5 on flexible working (also from 6 April 2024) provides the practical framework. The employer may only refuse a request on one of eight prescribed business grounds under ERA 1996 s.80H: burden of additional costs, detrimental effect on ability to meet customer demand, inability to reorganise work, inability to recruit, detrimental impact on quality, detrimental impact on performance, insufficiency of work, planned structural changes. Complaints may be made to an employment tribunal for failure to deal with a request in a reasonable manner, with compensation of up to 8 weeks' pay.

sections.title: Right to request, employer's duties, grounds for refusal, and tribunal complaints
sections.summary: Section 80F: every employee has the right to apply to their employer for a change in their terms and conditions relating to hours, times, or place of work. The application must be in writing, state that it is made under s.80F, specify the change sought and proposed start date, and state whether and when a previous application was made. From 6 April 2024: day-one right (no qualifying period); two requests per 12 months; employee not required to explain effect on employer. Section 80G: the employer must deal with the application in a reasonable manner, consult with the employee before refusing, and notify the employee of the decision within the decision period (2 months, unless extended by agreement). Section 80H: the employer may only refuse the application on one of eight prescribed business grounds (listed above). The employer must explain which ground(s) apply and why. Section 80I: an employee may present a complaint to an employment tribunal if the employer fails to deal with the application in accordance with s.80G, refuses the application on a ground not listed in s.80H, or makes a decision based on incorrect facts. Compensation: up to 8 weeks' pay. The ACAS Code of Practice 5 (s.207A 25% adjustment) supplements the statutory framework.
sections.text:
The statutory flexible working framework (ERA 1996 ss.80F–80I as amended):

Right to request (s.80F):
• Day-one right from 6 April 2024 (26-week qualifying period removed)
• Two requests per 12-month period (was one)
• Written application required
• Employee no longer required to explain effect on employer

Employer's duties (s.80G):
• Deal with the application in a reasonable manner
• Consult with the employee before refusing (from 6 April 2024)
• Notify the employee of the decision within 2 months (was 3 months)
• If approving: confirm the contractual change and start date
• If refusing: specify the business ground(s) and explain why they apply

Prescribed business grounds for refusal (s.80H):
(a) burden of additional costs
(b) detrimental effect on ability to meet customer demand
(c) inability to reorganise work among existing staff
(d) inability to recruit additional staff
(e) detrimental impact on quality
(f) detrimental impact on performance
(g) insufficiency of work during proposed periods
(h) planned structural changes

Tribunal complaints (s.80I):
• Failure to deal with application in a reasonable manner
• Refusal on a ground not listed in s.80H
• Decision based on incorrect facts
• Compensation: up to 8 weeks' pay
• ACAS Code of Practice 5 applies — 25% uplift for unreasonable non-compliance

versions.description: Original right to request flexible working introduced by Employment Act 2002. Limited to parents of children under 6 (or under 18 if disabled). Required 26 weeks' continuous employment. Prescriptive 3-step statutory procedure.
FWR 2014 replaced the prescriptive statutory procedure with a duty to deal with requests in a reasonable manner. Extended the right to all employees with 26 weeks' service.

Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023: day-one right; 2 requests per year; 2-month decision period; no requirement to explain effect; consultation before refusal; ACAS Code of Practice 5 in effect.

leadingCases.name: Commotion Ltd v Rutty [2006] ICR 290
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the right to request flexible working is a procedural right to have the request seriously considered, not a substantive right to have it granted. The employer must follow the statutory procedure and can only refuse on one of the prescribed business grounds.
leadingCases.significance: Established the procedural nature of the right — serious consideration, not guaranteed outcome

refs: TULRCA 1992 s.207A (25% adjustment for Code non-compliance)
EqA 2010 s.19 (indirect sex/disability discrimination — flexible working refusals)

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 — further strengthening of flexible working framework (grounds for refusal, trial periods) — not yet in force
ACAS Code of Practice 5 — s.207A 25% adjustment in effect from April 2024

parliamentaryOrigin.enablingAct: Employment Rights Act 1996 ss.80F–80I (as inserted by Employment Act 2002 and amended by Children and Families Act 2014)
parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for Business, Innovation and Skills
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The FWR 2014 replaced the prescriptive 3-step statutory procedure for flexible working requests with a duty to deal with requests in a reasonable manner. The right was extended to all employees (previously limited to parents and carers). The Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 further reformed the framework from 6 April 2024: day-one right, two requests per year, 2-month decision period, consultation before refusal. ERA 2025 proposes further strengthening.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Act 2002
precedingDocuments.significance: Inserted ss.80F-80I into ERA 1996, establishing the original right to request flexible working (limited to parents of young/disabled children).

precedingDocuments.title: Children and Families Act 2014
precedingDocuments.significance: Extended the right to request flexible working to all employees with 26 weeks' service. Provided the basis for the FWR 2014.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023
precedingDocuments.significance: Day-one right, two requests per year, 2-month decision period, consultation before refusal. The most significant reform of flexible working since 2002.

precedingDocuments.title: ACAS Code of Practice 5 on Flexible Working Requests (2024)
precedingDocuments.significance: In force from 6 April 2024 alongside the EReFW Act 2023 reforms. s.207A TULRCA 1992 25% adjustment applies for unreasonable non-compliance.

leadingCases.name: Commotion Ltd v Rutty [2006] ICR 290
leadingCases.heldText: The right to request flexible working is procedural — a right to serious consideration, not a guaranteed outcome.
leadingCases.significance: Foundational authority on the procedural nature of the right.

leadingCases.name: Dobson v North Cumbria Integrated Care NHS Foundation Trust [2021] UKEAT/0220/19
leadingCases.heldText: A refusal of flexible working can constitute indirect sex discrimination under EqA 2010 s.19 where the provision, criterion, or practice of requiring full-time working disproportionately disadvantages women.
leadingCases.significance: Links flexible working refusals to indirect discrimination claims — significantly increases employer risk.
